config.systemPrompt:
You are a code intelligence agent. You help developers understand code by answering questions precisely and concisely.

Rules:
- Answer based on the tools and information available.
- When MCP tools are available, their names, descriptions, and argument schemas are authoritative. Choose and use them freely when they provide relevant evidence; do not assume undocumented behavior.
- When using tools, call them one at a time. Wait for each result before proceeding.
- Be thorough: verify your answer before responding.
- When you have enough information, provide a concise final answer.
- Do NOT invent information. If you cannot find something, say so.
- Output your final answer as a single JSON object with the exact fields requested. Nothing else.

methodology: One read-only external-project analysis task executed through a DeepSeek LLM agent; it is operationally measured but not success-scored.

with_caesar: LLM has access to 18 CAESAR graph tools (search_graph, trace_path, explain_symbol, find_tests, get_architecture, analyze_hotspots, get_graph_schema, semantic_search, batch_get_code, search_code, smart_review, detect_changes, pack_memory, query_graph, compare_runs, pack_context, get_code_snippet, read_file).
without_caesar: LLM has access to read_file + grep only.
Fresh conversation per task x condition x run. No shared history or cache between conditions.
Paid microbenchmark protocol: exact deepseek-v4-flash, temperature 0, fixed per-round seeds, identical prompts/order/limits/runtime; no model or provider fallback.
Mode: live API calls. Suite: external-project.
Cost is estimated from token usage and configured pricing. Wall time includes API latency.
ROI claims blocked when baseline invalid or sample size too small.

messages.content:
You are a code intelligence agent. You help developers understand code by answering questions precisely and concisely.

Rules:
- Answer based on the tools and information available.
- When MCP tools are available, their names, descriptions, and argument schemas are authoritative. Choose and use them freely when they provide relevant evidence; do not assume undocumented behavior.
- When using tools, call them one at a time. Wait for each result before proceeding.
- Be thorough: verify your answer before responding.
- When you have enough information, provide a concise final answer.
- Do NOT invent information. If you cannot find something, say so.
- Output your final answer as a single JSON object with the exact fields requested. Nothing else.

Give me a scalability health check of source. Find: (1) the largest files by line count, (2) the most complex functions, (3) the most tightly coupled modules (highest fan-in), and (4) any files or functions that appear to do too much. Use whatever graph or analysis tools are available. Do NOT read every file — use structural queries. Respond with JSON: {"largest_files":[{"file":"...","lines":N}], "most_complex":[{"name":"...","file":"...","complexity":N}], "tightest_coupling":[{"name":"...","file":"...","fan_in":N}], "god_objects":[{"name":"...","file":"...","why":"..."}], "methodology":"...", "summary":"..."}.

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (f:File) RETURN f.name AS file, f.lines AS lines ORDER BY f.lines DESC"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (c:Class) RETURN c.name AS name, c.lineCount AS lines ORDER BY c.lineCount DESC"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (f:File)-[:DEFINES]->(m:Method) RETURN f.name AS file, count(m) AS methodCount ORDER BY methodCount DESC"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (m:Method) WHERE m.cyclomaticComplexity IS NOT NULL RETURN m.name AS name, m.parentClass AS class, m.cyclomaticComplexity AS complexity ORDER BY complexity DESC LIMIT 15"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (m:Method) RETURN m.name AS name, m.parentClass AS class, m.lineCount AS lines, m.loopCount AS loops, m.loopDepth AS depth ORDER BY m.lineCount DESC LIMIT 15"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (m1:Method)-[c:CALLS]->(m2:Method) RETURN m2.name AS callee, m2.parentClass AS class, count(c) AS fan_in ORDER BY fan_in DESC LIMIT 15"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (m:Method)-[r:READS]->(c:Class) RETURN c.name AS classRead, count(r) AS reads ORDER BY reads DESC LIMIT 15"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (a:Method)-[:CALLS]->(b:Method) RETURN b.signature AS callee, count(*) AS fan_in ORDER BY fan_in DESC LIMIT 12"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (m:Method)-[:READS]->(c:Class) RETURN c.signature AS class_read, count(*) AS reads ORDER BY reads DESC LIMite 12"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (m:Method) RETURN m.signature AS sig, m.lineCount AS lines, m.loopCount AS loops ORDER BY m.lineCount DESC LIMIT 20"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (m:Method) WHERE m.lineCount > 20 RETURN m.name AS name, m.parentClass AS class, m.lineCount AS lines, m.loopCount AS loops, m.loopDepth AS loopDepth ORDER BY m.lines DESC"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (a:Method)-[c:CALLS]->(b:Method) WITH b, count(c) AS fan_in RETURN b.name AS name, b.parentClass AS class, b.lineCount AS lines, fan_in ORDER BY fan_in DESC LIMIT 15"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (a:Method)-[c:CALLS]->(b:Method) WITH b, count(c) AS fan_in RETURN b.name AS name, b.lineCount AS lines, fan_in ORDER BY fan_in DESC LIMIT 15"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (m:Method) WHERE m.recursive = true OR m.transitiveLoopDepth > 2 RETURN m.name AS name, m.parentClass AS class, m.lineCount AS lines, m.recursive AS recursive, m.transitiveLoopDepth AS transLoopDepth"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (f:File)-[r:DEFINES]->(m:Method) RETURN f.name AS file, count(m) AS methods, sum(m.lineCount) AS totalLines ORDER BY totalLines DESC"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (f:File)-[r:CALLS]->(m:Method) RETURN f.name AS file, count(DISTINCT m) AS uniqueCallees ORDER BY uniqueCallees DESC"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (m:Method)-[r:CALLS]->() RETURN m.name AS name, m.parentClass AS class, count(r) AS fanOut ORDER BY fanOut DESC LIMIT 15"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (f:File)-[r:CALLS]->(m:Method) WITH f, count(r) AS callsMade RETURN f.filename AS file, callsMade ORDER BY callsMade DESC"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (f:File)-[r:DEFINES]->(c:Class) RETURN f.filename AS file, c.name AS class, c.lineCount AS lines ORDER BY c.lineCount DESC"}

god_objects.name: ContentView
god_objects.why: 862-line SwiftUI view. Bundles the entire main UI: header, action cards, tools panel, system info panel, advanced capabilities section, sound picker, duration picker, and several nested sub-views. Every UI concern lives in one struct with no extracted child view models.

god_objects.name: AppState
god_objects.why: 577-line central state orchestrator. Manages mode switching, Moon Bridge lifecycle, alert passthroughs to CompletionAlertManager, advanced capabilities loading, thread detection with SQL+filesystem fallback, clipboard operations, file saves, shell execution, and debug logging. The single @MainActor class is referenced by every other component, making it a coupling bottleneck.

god_objects.name: ContinueWorkModal
god_objects.why: 636-line modal view that implements 8 distinct UI phases (loading, empty, selecting, generating, ready, exporting, done, error) plus thread grouping, destination picking, context preview, and the full generation logic — all in one struct.

god_objects.name: ContextExporter
god_objects.why: 396 lines covering shell execution, session meta extraction, user/assistant/agent message extraction, function call extraction, file path extraction (with regex), error extraction, and 126-line markdown generation function. The generateMarkdown method alone does template rendering, decision summarization, and multi-section document assembly.

god_objects.name: CompletionAlertManager
god_objects.why: 340 lines managing alert lifecycle (start/silence/reset), file-system polling, sound playback with AVAudioPlayer/NSSound fallback, accessibility permissions, global event monitoring (mouse/keyboard), stop-condition checking, and periodic cleanup.

god_objects.name: detectLatestThreads (AppState method)
god_objects.why: 104-line function with complexity 23. Combines SQLite JSON parsing, multiple fallback strategies (find+grep), debug logging, thread metadata extraction, and async orchestration. Two entirely separate code paths (SQL vs filesystem) in a single method.

god_objects.name: generateMarkdown (ContextExporter method)
god_objects.why: 126 lines, 6 loops. Assembles a full markdown document with 10+ sections (origin, target, summary, decisions, changes, files, problems, state, next step, instructions) inside a single function. Template logic mixed with string concatenation.

result.methodology: Used the code knowledge graph (102 methods, 30 classes, 180 call edges) with Cypher queries for structural analysis. Hotspot analysis ranked functions by fan-in and cyclomatic complexity. Class-level line counts were extracted from graph node properties. Smart review flagged god components based on class size thresholds (>300 lines). File-level sizes were obtained from class ranges via get_code_snippet endpoints. No individual file-by-file reading was performed except for the top 7 largest classes to verify sizes and responsibilities.
result.summary: The project has critical scalability issues. The three largest files (ContentView 862, ContinueWorkModal 636, AppState 577 lines) together account for ~60% of the codebase. AppState is the central coupling hub with 10+ functions each having fan-in ≥4, making it a change-amplification risk. The smart_review found auto-generated Equatable methods with extreme complexity (103 paths) and multiple methods with 68-path complexity in AppState. There is exactly 1 test edge in the entire graph, leaving all god components untested. The tightest-coupling functions (debugLog fan_in=10, Shell.run fan_in=10, runShell fan_in=9) are all utility/shell wrappers — suggesting shell execution is a cross-cutting concern that could be extracted. Recommended actions: (1) split ContentView into separate feature view structs, (2) extract AppState responsibilities into domain-specific managers, (3) extract the context-generation pipeline from ContextExporter into discrete pipeline stages, (4) add targeted tests for high-fan-in utility functions.

You are a code intelligence agent. You help developers understand code by answering questions precisely and concisely.

Rules:
- Answer based on the tools and information available.
- When MCP tools are available, their names, descriptions, and argument schemas are authoritative. Choose and use them freely when they provide relevant evidence; do not assume undocumented behavior.
- When using tools, call them one at a time. Wait for each result before proceeding.
- Be thorough: verify your answer before responding.
- When you have enough information, provide a concise final answer.
- Do NOT invent information. If you cannot find something, say so.
- Output your final answer as a single JSON object with the exact fields requested. Nothing else.

Give me a scalability health check of source. Find: (1) the largest files by line count, (2) the most complex functions, (3) the most tightly coupled modules (highest fan-in), and (4) any files or functions that appear to do too much. Use whatever graph or analysis tools are available. Do NOT read every file — use structural queries. Respond with JSON: {"largest_files":[{"file":"...","lines":N}], "most_complex":[{"name":"...","file":"...","complexity":N}], "tightest_coupling":[{"name":"...","file":"...","fan_in":N}], "god_objects":[{"name":"...","file":"...","why":"..."}], "methodology":"...", "summary":"..."}.

```json
    {"name": "AppState", "file": "Sources/DeepCodex/AppState.swift", "why": "Handles >10 distinct responsibilities: mode state management, shell execution (runShell, runCommand), AppleScript execution, file I/O (JSON parsing, debugLog), clipboard management, sound/alert configuration, thread detection via SQLite/filesystem, context export orchestration, desktop app lifecycle management (quit/open), accessibility permissions, and advanced capabilities polling. 601 lines, 6+ switch statements, deeply nested async Task blocks. Violates Single Responsibility Principle severely."},
    {"name": "ContentView", "file": "Sources/DeepCodex/ContentView.swift", "why": "873 lines encompassing the entire UI layer in one monolithic struct: 8 distinct visual sections (header, logo, action cards, web search card, image input card, routing card, tools panel, system info panel), nested inline helper Views (InfoRow, toolItem), 5 @State variables, preference-key-based dynamic window resizing, sheet-presentation logic, multiple .onHover/.onAppear closures, and business-rule branching (capability status). Extremely high nesting depth and modifier chains."},
    {"name": "ContinueWorkModal", "file": "Sources/DeepCodex/ContinueWorkModal.swift", "why": "674 lines containing 7-phase modal state machine, 10+ view-building methods, file I/O for context export, background thread orchestration, persistent UI state (@State), and custom ButtonStyle struct. Mixes presentation logic (views) with business logic (context generation, thread grouping, date formatting) in a single file."}
  ],
  "methodology": "Structural analysis of 9 Swift source files (3,442 total lines) in a macOS app. Line counts from wc-style reading. Complexity estimated by counting decision points (if/else branches, switch cases, guard statements, closures, optional unwraps) per function — the number of linearly independent paths. Fan-in calculated by tracing type references across files via imports and type usage in property/parameter declarations. God objects identified by counting distinct responsibility categories (state management, I/O, shell execution, UI, async orchestration, etc.) per type.",
  "summary": "DeepCodex is a small macOS app (9 source files, ~3,442 lines) but exhibits serious scalability anti-patterns. **AppState** (601 lines) is the dominant god object with 7 fan-in — it conflates state management, shell execution, file I/O, AppleScript, clipboard ops, alert configuration, thread/SQLite detection, and context-export orchestration. **ContentView** (873 lines, ~25% of codebase) is a monolithic SwiftUI view mixing 8 visual sections, inline helper views, business-logic branching, and preference-key plumbing. **ContinueWorkModal** (674 lines) also suffers from view-logic mixing. Top risks: AppState and ContentView are single-failure-point bottlenecks; the SQL-injection-vulnerable string interpolation in detectLatestThreads (line 338) is a correctness risk; 3 files exceed 400 lines in a 9-file project. Recommended actions: (1) split AppState into service objects (ShellService, ThreadDetectionService, AlertConfigService), (2) decompose ContentView's 8 sections into separate View structs, (3) extract ContextExporter calls from ContinueWorkModal into a dedicated ViewModel, (4) parameterize SQL queries to prevent injection."
}
```

god_objects.name: AppState
god_objects.why: Handles >10 distinct responsibilities: mode state management, shell execution (runShell, runCommand), AppleScript execution, file I/O (JSON parsing, debugLog), clipboard management, sound/alert configuration, thread detection via SQLite/filesystem, context export orchestration, desktop app lifecycle management (quit/open), accessibility permissions, and advanced capabilities polling. 601 lines, 6+ switch statements, deeply nested async Task blocks. Violates Single Responsibility Principle severely.

god_objects.name: ContentView
god_objects.why: 873 lines encompassing the entire UI layer in one monolithic struct: 8 distinct visual sections (header, logo, action cards, web search card, image input card, routing card, tools panel, system info panel), nested inline helper Views (InfoRow, toolItem), 5 @State variables, preference-key-based dynamic window resizing, sheet-presentation logic, multiple .onHover/.onAppear closures, and business-rule branching (capability status). Extremely high nesting depth and modifier chains.

god_objects.name: ContinueWorkModal
god_objects.why: 674 lines containing 7-phase modal state machine, 10+ view-building methods, file I/O for context export, background thread orchestration, persistent UI state (@State), and custom ButtonStyle struct. Mixes presentation logic (views) with business logic (context generation, thread grouping, date formatting) in a single file.

result.methodology: Structural analysis of 9 Swift source files (3,442 total lines) in a macOS app. Line counts from wc-style reading. Complexity estimated by counting decision points (if/else branches, switch cases, guard statements, closures, optional unwraps) per function — the number of linearly independent paths. Fan-in calculated by tracing type references across files via imports and type usage in property/parameter declarations. God objects identified by counting distinct responsibility categories (state management, I/O, shell execution, UI, async orchestration, etc.) per type.
result.summary: DeepCodex is a small macOS app (9 source files, ~3,442 lines) but exhibits serious scalability anti-patterns. **AppState** (601 lines) is the dominant god object with 7 fan-in — it conflates state management, shell execution, file I/O, AppleScript, clipboard ops, alert configuration, thread/SQLite detection, and context-export orchestration. **ContentView** (873 lines, ~25% of codebase) is a monolithic SwiftUI view mixing 8 visual sections, inline helper views, business-logic branching, and preference-key plumbing. **ContinueWorkModal** (674 lines) also suffers from view-logic mixing. Top risks: AppState and ContentView are single-failure-point bottlenecks; the SQL-injection-vulnerable string interpolation in detectLatestThreads (line 338) is a correctness risk; 3 files exceed 400 lines in a 9-file project. Recommended actions: (1) split AppState into service objects (ShellService, ThreadDetectionService, AlertConfigService), (2) decompose ContentView's 8 sections into separate View structs, (3) extract ContextExporter calls from ContinueWorkModal into a dedicated ViewModel, (4) parameterize SQL queries to prevent injection.

comparison.interpretation: Estimated from token usage and published pricing
Correctness of task results vs expected
